chapters.title: Five children, one struggling mother, no safe ground
chapters.text:
Regina Calcaterra has four siblings named Rosie, Norm, Camille, and Cherie. All five share different fathers and grew up under their mother, Cookie, on Long Island.

Stability was a stranger in their home. Cookie was always on the run from police, wanted for a string of crimes, and the family bounced from place to place. One night they might sleep in a shelter. The next, they could be curled up in a car in some parking lot.

Regina never felt that her mother loved her. She suspected Cookie always favored the older girls over her.

Alongside the constant moves and uncertainty, affection was rare. Cookie could be cruel and icy one moment, then swing into manic glee the next. She would vanish for weeks, leaving the kids to fend for themselves. The three oldest daughters, Regina, Camille, and Cherie, made a silent pact to keep the family together and look after one another.

In one vivid memory, the family piled into their battered old car and crossed Long Island. Their few belongings sat in plastic bags in the trunk. The doors no longer worked, so the children climbed in and out through the windows. After a long drive along back roads, they pulled up to a house that finally looked decent.

Kids with careless parents often grow up too fast, taking on duties meant for adults, like caring for younger siblings.

Cookie waved the kids inside, and Regina wondered how long this new place would last. Any hope drained away when Cookie disappeared for six long weeks, leaving the children to beg for food, wash cars for coins, and struggle to stay clean.

This summary walks through Regina's story with her brothers and sisters, the desperate fight to keep the family together, and the endless hurdles they faced. By the end, you may find yourself pulling your own family close and giving quiet thanks for the bonds you share.

chapters.title: Abuse and a social worker at the door
chapters.text:
Regina remembers her mother as someone who was always angry and cold. Cookie cared only about her own needs and had no idea how to offer warmth. She flared up fast and took it out on all five kids. The youngest, Rosie, once dropped a glass by accident. The shattering noise set Cookie off. She grabbed Rosie, screamed horrible names at her, and slammed her down onto the broken shards. It was not the first time, and it was not the last.

Regina Calcaterra has written that the hurt, the anger, and the constant rejection were her only steady companions through almost fourteen years with Cookie.

Most children feel safe at home, but Regina and her siblings never did. Covering up bruises became a skill they practiced until they were sharp at it. When a teacher at school noticed marks on Regina's body and asked where they came from, the children ran through their list of excuses. They had tripped on the stairs, bumped into a door, and so on.

One afternoon, Regina came home to find her mother gone and a strange car in the driveway. A social worker was waiting. The children's usual lies could not explain the injuries this time, and the social worker told them they would be removed for their own safety.

As the kids were led to the social services cars, Regina burned with shame. Despite everything she had tried, she had failed to keep her family whole.

Foster care split them up. Norm and Rosie went to one home, while Regina and Camille went to another. Cherie had already left a few months earlier, after the birth of her first child. Regina and Camille learned quickly that foster children carry the label of trouble until they prove otherwise.

For many kids in care, foster homes are not about finding belonging. They are about finding someone willing to watch over them.

chapters.title: Cookie charmed the system, then fell apart again
chapters.text:
The children spent months in foster care before their mother turned up again. Of course, Cookie came armed with tall tales about a brand new life. She claimed she had a steady job at a real estate agency and a new boyfriend named Karl. Regina saw through every word, but social services bought the story. The kids went back to Cookie and her new partner.

Things stayed calm for a few weeks. Then the children came home from school to find the house boarded up and their belongings dumped on the lawn. Cookie had failed to pay rent again, and the family was homeless once more. Soon she started drinking heavily, and the abuse picked up where it had left off.

Abuse of children often grows worse with alcohol or drugs in the mix.

The kids returned to separate foster homes. A few months later, Regina heard that Cookie had won back custody of Rosie and Norm. The court decided she could handle two children first, and if she proved herself, the others would follow. To drown out the chaos, Regina threw herself into school. Before long, she and Camille were sent back to their mother. Once again, they were moving from place to place, with a parade of men drifting in and out of Cookie's life.

Exhausted by the chaos, Regina made the painful call to seek emancipation. It came just four days after Cookie flew into a rage, kicking her and cutting her with shards of broken glass. Enough was enough.

Rosie and Norm went to a different foster home. The older siblings were allowed a weekly call with them. Rosie told Regina that her new foster parents were harsh and angry. They hit her when she misbehaved. She told the social worker, who replied that Rosie and Norm needed to behave better. Regina felt helpless, abandoned by the very system meant to protect them.

Worth knowing: emancipation is the legal process that lets a child become a legal adult before turning eighteen, cutting them free from an abusive or controlling parent.

chapters.title: A door slammed in her face, once again
chapters.text:
After giving up on her mother, Regina tried to find her father. She knew his name was Paul Accerbi, but she had no idea how to track him down. She wanted to reach out and prove that even though she had been raised in foster care, she was still a child he could be proud of.

She flipped through the phone book, but every lead went cold. She remembered distant relatives with the same last name, so she wrote to them and asked if they would pass along a note to Paul. Then the long wait began.

Finally, a man called and asked for her, but she was at school. She waited by the phone, hoping he would want to meet her face to face.

Many children abandoned by a parent just want recognition. They want a face to match the shape in their imagination.

When the phone rang again, Regina picked up. Her heart sank as Paul told her that her letter had brought him nothing but grief. He said his wife had been made sick with worry. He denied being her father and begged her not to contact him again. Yet Regina knew he had gone out of his way to find her number, or he would not have called back. She was certain he was her father. She promised herself she would give him a little more time.

He did call back. He explained that Cookie had been very promiscuous and that their meeting was a single night, so he was most likely not her father. Regina fired back with details only Paul would know. If it was only one night, how did Cookie know so much about him? She was furious that he would not give her a chance, when all she wanted was to show him she was a good kid, not the stereotype of a foster child.

chapters.title: An attempt to rescue Rosie that failed
chapters.text:
After failing to reach the man she believed was her father, Regina poured herself into the one thing she could control, her studies. She was legally free of her mother, so she was caught off guard when Cookie called close to her graduation and said she had a gift to give her. Regina agreed to meet her in Idaho, where Cookie was living with Rosie.

The moment she arrived, Regina saw that nothing had really changed. Cookie was the same underneath, and leaving Rosie behind broke her heart. Her mood lifted a bit when she learned she had been accepted into college.

Regina and Camille worried about Rosie while trying to build their own lives. Camille was married with a baby named Frankie, and the two sisters called Rosie often to check on her. They heard about a fresh incident with social services that sent Cookie into a rage. Rosie was hurt.

Children of abusive parents simply want a normal, warm home, free from chaos and fear.

Deciding they had to act, Regina and Camille tried to bring Rosie to live with Camille, her husband, and her son. Rosie arrived happy and hopeful for a normal life, a steady school, and new friends. Then Cookie's brother, Nick, got in touch. He threatened to call the police unless they returned Rosie to him, so she could be sent back to Cookie.

Regina was sure that explaining everything to social services would keep Rosie safe. Her hope shattered when she called their social worker and laid out the case. The social worker brushed them off, saying she could not help because they had taken Rosie across state lines. They had no choice but to send Rosie back to Idaho, and back to Cookie.

The older sisters felt abandoned by the system and crushed by the sense that they had let their youngest sister down.

chapters.title: Rising past the limits placed on foster children
chapters.text:
Regina was still raw from the fight to save Rosie, and disappointed by the entire social work system. She decided the best path forward was to focus on her education and her career. She wanted to break the stereotype that foster children were doomed to repeat their parents' lives.

So she started looking for work after graduation. Her strong grades and steady confidence helped her land a government job at the New York State office. She quickly made her mark, helping push several new laws through the state. Her boss saw her potential and put her on bigger projects, including speaking directly to the White House on immigration policy.

Many foster children lose every form of support the moment they turn eighteen. Regina Calcaterra showed that hard work and grit can break that cycle.

Her success gave her fresh courage. She reached out to Paul Accerbi again, this time asking him to take a DNA test to settle the question of paternity once and for all. She told him she did not want money or anything from him. She only wanted to know. She could stand on her own two feet and did not need his support. He refused to take the test, and she had to let it rest for a while.

Her career kept climbing. She passed the New York State bar exam. Around that time, she realized she could make a real difference for children who had grown up the way she had. She threw herself into activism for foster children, raising awareness and pushing for new programs.

Around the same time, she finally received something she had wanted for years, a complete birth certificate with both parents' names on it. Paul Accerbi eventually signed a court judgment admitting he knew of their connection, even as he refused to accept full paternity.

chapters.title: A story of pain that ends in quiet hope
chapters.text:
Etched in Sand is a remarkable story. It is heartbreaking, yet it shines a harsh light on the cracks in the foster care system and the way so many children get failed by the very people paid to help them.

No child should grow up feeling unwanted, let alone face physical and emotional abuse. Cookie was mentally unwell, an alcoholic, and deeply unstable. Her five children never had a real chance while they were in her care. Despite constant efforts to stay together, they were separated time and again and forced to live in different cities and states.

There is grief in this story, but there is also a sliver of hope. Regina Calcaterra does not have to be the exception. She has built a successful life and helped drive real change for others. Yet she does not have to remain an outlier. She can become the rule. That will only happen when the social work system itself changes.

Older foster children are less likely to be adopted. When they reach adulthood, they are pushed out on their own. They get a thin layer of guidance on how to live independently, and too many end up homeless, falling back into chaotic patterns that mirror their parents. With more help and more support, that does not have to be the case. Many more children can build lives like the one Regina carved out for herself.

Every year in the United States, about forty thousand foster children age out of the system with nowhere to go and no one to help them, as Regina Calcaterra has written.

Give this a try.

It is easy to judge people in care. Try setting those judgments aside and offering a hand instead.

Stay alert to the signs of child abuse. Too many children suffer at the hands of a parent, and the people around them often miss the warning signs.

When hard times come, throw yourself into something positive, whether it is your studies or your work. That is what Regina Calcaterra did, and it helped her find her way forward.
